title: The Wonderful Adventures of Nils
author: Selma Lagerlöf
quotesVerified: The Wonderful Adventures of Nils, Velma Swanston Howard's translation, Project Gutenberg #10935. Quotes preserve the source's inconsistent spellings ('Skane', 'Gottland').
overture: A lazy farm boy in the far south of Sweden is shrunk to thumb-size, and carried off on the back of a white gander when the wild geese fly north for the spring. From Skåne to Lapland and home again, he sees the whole length of his country from the air - and is made kind by it.
sources: The journey is a curated spine of the flock's real, named landfalls - Vittskövle, Glimmingehus, Kullaberg, Karlskrona, Öland, Gotland, Skansen, Kebnekaise - out of the dozens of provinces the book visits as a geography primer.

The migration is drawn as flight: a bird crosses land and sea alike, so the routes are illustrative arcs following the book's south-to-north-and-home arc, not surveyed lines. Vineta, the sunken city, is a legend and is not pinned; Visby stands for the Gotland landfall.

regions.name: The Blekinge coast and the Baltic islands

chapters.title: The Great Crane Dance on Kullaberg

characters.name: Nils Holgersson
characters.role: The idle farm boy, shrunk to thumb-size, who is carried the length of Sweden on a gander's back and comes home kind.

characters.name: Mårten the gander
characters.role: The big white farm goose who springs up to join the wild flock, carrying Nils; the one tame bird among them.

characters.name: Akka of Kebnekaise
characters.role: The ancient grey lead goose, the flock's wisdom and its moral centre, whose home is the far northern mountain.

exit.note: On the ninth of November Nils goes down to the shore to see the flock off, human again and no longer able to speak or understand bird. Akka lands once to say goodbye, and then the wedge flies silently out over the Baltic for as long as he can follow it with his eyes. The book never says where she winters.

characters.name: Smirre the fox
characters.role: The fox who stalks the flock overland across the south, until he breaks the animals' truce and is cast out for good.

exit.note: Smirre breaks the crane-dance truce the hour he arrives, and the other foxes sentence him to exile, bite off his right earlap and chase him off the headland and out of Skåne. The book does pick him up again much later, far to the north and finally loose on an unnamed island out in the ocean, but none of that is on the southern thread this map draws, and he is certainly not standing on Kullaberg for the rest of the year.

locations.name: Västra Vemmenhög, Skåne
locations.note: Nils's home farm, in a real hamlet in southernmost Skåne; the village is named and locatable, the particular farm commemorated today as Nils Holgerssongården.
locations.story: The flat Scanian farm where an idle boy torments a tomte, is shrunk to thumb-size, and is swept off after the wild geese - and where, one November, he lands again and is made whole.
locations.quote: the boy lived in West Vemminghög Township, down in Southern Skane, where the spring was already in full swing.

image.caption: A Scanian farm cottage among its trees, drawn 1924 - an indicative view of the humble southern farmland where Nils begins, and ends.
image.credit: Ferdinand Boberg, 1924 · Nordiska museet · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Vittskövle, Skåne
locations.note: A real moated Renaissance castle in eastern Skåne, near which the wild flock first lights.
locations.story: The meadow by Vittskövle manor where the wild geese roost and Akka first weighs the tame gander and the useless boy - and where the fox Smirre makes his first raid.
locations.quote: A flock of wild geese came flying one morning, and lit on a meadow down in Eastern Skåne not very far from Vittskövle manor.

image.caption: Vittskövle castle, engraved 1680 - the moated Renaissance manor by whose meadows the wild flock first lights.
image.credit: After Gerhard von Burman, 1680 (published by Abraham Fischer, 1756) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Glimmingehus, Skåne
locations.note: A real medieval stone keep near Hammenhög in south-eastern Skåne - the best-preserved in Scandinavia.
locations.story: The grim old keep where storks nest on the roof and grey rats have overrun the halls, until Nils pipes the whole horde of them out of the gates and away.
locations.quote: In south-eastern Skåne--not far from the sea there is an old castle called Glimminge.

image.caption: Glimmingehus, engraved 1680 - the great medieval stone keep from which Nils pipes the rats.
image.credit: After Gerhard von Burman, 1680 (published by Abraham Fischer, 1756) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Kullaberg, Skåne
locations.note: A real headland ridge on the Skåne coast, marked at its cape by Kullen lighthouse; the crane dance is set on its broad heath, anchored here at the point.
locations.story: The headland where, once a year, the animals keep a truce to watch the grey cranes dance - and where Smirre the fox breaks the peace, and is cast out for it.
locations.quote: Although there are many magnificent buildings in Skåne, it must be acknowledged that there's not one among them that has such pretty walls as old Kullaberg.

image.caption: The lighthouse on Kullen, the Kullaberg cape in Skåne - a 19th-century engraving of the headland where the animals keep their yearly truce.
image.credit: 'Fyrtornet på Kullen i Skåne', 19th-century wood-engraving · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Karlskrona, Blekinge
locations.note: The real naval city of Blekinge; the bronze statue of King Karl XI stands on Stortorget, the wooden Rosenbom poor-box by the Admiralty Church.
locations.story: The moonlit naval town where cheeky Nils mocks the bronze king on the square - who climbs down and chases him through the streets, the wooden almsman Rosenbom caught up in the hunt.
locations.quote: It was a moonlight evening in Karlskrona--calm and beautiful.

image.caption: Stortorget, Karlskrona, 1901 - the great square and the bronze statue of King Karl XI, who climbs down to chase Nils through the streets.
image.credit: Gustav Adolph Johnson, stereograph, 1901 · Riksantikvarieämbetet · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Ottenby, Öland
locations.note: The real royal demesne of Ottenby at the southern cape of the island of Öland, marked by the Långe Jan lighthouse - a famous staging ground for migrating birds.
locations.story: The bare southern heath of Öland, where the flock rests among a great gathering of migrating birds before the leap out over the open Baltic.
locations.quote: On the most southerly part of Öland lies a royal demesne, which is called Ottenby.
locations.imageBlank: No clean public-domain period view of Öland's southern point (Ottenby / the Långe Jan lighthouse) surfaced - the available photographs are all modern and licensed. Better a logged blank than a doubtful one.

locations.name: Visby, Gotland
locations.note: The real walled town of Visby on Gotland - 'the city of ruins and roses'. Its counterpart in the chapter, the drowned city of Vineta, is a legend and is not given coordinates.
locations.story: The walled island town where Nils walks the streets of Vineta, a sunken city risen for one hour in a hundred years, and cannot find the coin that would save it - so it sinks again, and living Visby stands.
locations.quote: then they are more happy over the Visby that exists, than over a magnificent Vineta at the bottom of the sea.

image.caption: Visby from the north, watercolour c.1820 - the walled 'city of ruins and roses' on Gotland, set against the legend of drowned Vineta.
image.credit: Lars Cedergren, c.1816–1830 · Riksantikvarieämbetet · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Skansen, Stockholm
locations.note: The real open-air museum on Djurgården in Stockholm, founded 1891 - 'the great park just outside of Stockholm'.
locations.story: The park where Sweden is gathered in miniature, and where Nils is caught and held; by night the still figures wake, and he wins the friendship of the eagle who will one day carry him back to the geese.
locations.quote: A few years ago, at Skansen--the great park just outside of Stockholm where they have collected so many wonderful things--there lived a little old man, named Clement Larsson.

image.caption: Stockholm seen from the heights of Skansen, painted 1889 - the great park just outside the capital where Nils is caught and held.
image.credit: Karl Nordström, 1889 · Nationalmuseum · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Kebnekaise, Lappland
locations.note: Sweden's highest mountain, in Lapland - the named home of Akka and the wild flock. The geese summer across the fells broadly; the peak is the single mappable anchor.
locations.story: The roof of Sweden, above the glaciers in the Lapland light, where the wild geese come down at last to nest and raise their young through the brief bright summer.
locations.quote: He had stood on snow-capped Mount Kebnekaise, had looked down at the glaciers and visited many high cliffs seldom tramped by human feet.

image.caption: Roped climbers on the glacier ice of Kebnekaise, early 20th century - the snow-capped roof of Sweden where the wild geese summer.
image.credit: Ernst Herrmann, c.1900–1940 · Nordiska museet · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

movements.routeSource: The wild flock sweeps Nils north off the home farm to its first roost in eastern Skåne; drawn as a direct flight over the Scanian plain.
movements.note: Carried off after the wild geese, the length of southern Skåne.

movements.routeSource: A short hop south-east across Skåne to the coast; illustrative flight line.
movements.note: On to the stone keep by the sea.

movements.routeSource: West across the whole breadth of Skåne to the Kullen headland; illustrative flight line.
movements.note: Across Skåne to the crane dance.

movements.routeSource: East along the south coast into Blekinge; illustrative flight line.
Out over the water to the southern point of Öland; illustrative flight line over the sea.
Blown east over the open Baltic to Gotland; illustrative flight line over the sea.
North-west over the sea and the coast to the capital; illustrative flight line.
The long haul north over forest, lake and fell to Lapland; illustrative flight line up the length of the country.
movements.note: The long flight to the roof of Sweden.

movements.routeSource: The autumn return the length of Sweden, drawn down the western side as the book has it, back to the home farm; illustrative flight line.
movements.note: Home again, down the whole country in autumn.

movements.routeSource: Smirre follows the flock overland across Skåne, on his own four feet; illustrative ground line beneath the flight.
movements.note: The fox, running the ground below, keeps the geese in sight.

movements.routeSource: The fox's overland pursuit across Skåne to Kullaberg; illustrative ground line.
movements.note: On to Kullaberg, to the edge of the truce.

story.title: The boy who was shrunk
story.narration: In a farmyard in the far south of Sweden, an idle boy torments a tomte and is shrunk to thumb-size for it. When the wild geese pass over, the farm's white gander springs up to join them - and Nils, clinging to his neck, is swept into the sky.

story.title: Off with the wild geese
story.narration: The gander Mårten carries him north to the flock's first roost in eastern Skåne, led by the ancient grey goose Akka of Kebnekaise - who has no use at all for a tame bird or a useless boy.

story.title: Smirre's grudge
story.narration: In the night Smirre the fox creeps up on the sleeping geese. Nils wakes them just in time, and the fox is left with a single tail-feather and a grudge that will chase them clean across Skåne.

story.title: The stone keep
story.narration: On south-east to a grim old keep beside the sea - Glimmingehus, where the storks nest on the roof and something has gone badly wrong in the halls below.

story.title: The magic pipe
story.narration: Grey rats have overrun the castle. Nils borrows a little pipe and pipes the whole marching horde of them out of the gates and away - and for the first time the boy is not useless, but needed.

story.title: West to the headland
story.narration: West across the whole breadth of Skåne to the great cliff of Kullaberg, where once a year the animals keep a truce and gather to watch the cranes dance.

story.title: The crane dance
story.narration: On the heath the birds and beasts hold their yearly peace, crowned by the grave, wheeling dance of the grey cranes - the strangest and finest thing Nils has yet seen, from the air or the ground.
And the fox? Wind back. Smirre has not given up - all this while he has been running the ground below, keeping the flock in sight.

story.title: The ground pursuit
story.narration: Overland from the roost, across ploughland and beech-wood, always a field behind the geese.

story.title: To the edge of the truce
story.narration: On to Kullaberg itself, slinking up among the gathered animals to the very edge of their sacred peace.

story.title: Cast out
story.narration: There, maddened, Smirre breaks the truce to snatch at Nils - and for that the assembled animals cast him out for good. The flock is free of him at last.

story.title: East along the coast
story.narration: East along the shore into Blekinge, to the naval city of Karlskrona, asleep under the moon.

story.title: The bronze king
story.narration: Cheeky Nils mocks the bronze statue of old King Karl on the square - who climbs down in a fury and chases him through the streets, with the wooden almsman Rosenbom swept up into the hunt.

story.title: Out to Öland
story.narration: Out across the water to the long limestone island of Öland, and its bare southern heath where the migrating birds mass in their thousands.

story.title: The gathering of the birds
story.narration: At Ottenby, the old royal demesne at the island's foot, the flock rests among a vast gathering of birds before the leap out over the open Baltic.

story.title: Over the Baltic
story.narration: Blown east over the open sea to Gotland, and the walled town of Visby - the city of ruins and roses.

story.title: The sunken city
story.narration: Here Nils walks the streets of Vineta, a drowned city that rises for one hour in a hundred years; a shopman begs him to buy the smallest thing and break the spell, but the penniless boy cannot - and the city sinks again.

story.title: North to the capital
story.narration: North up the coast to Stockholm, the capital spread on its islands and its water.

story.title: Skansen wakes
story.narration: At Skansen, the park where all Sweden is gathered in miniature, Nils is caught and held; by night the still figures wake, and he wins a friend in the great eagle who will one day carry him back to the geese.

story.title: The long flight north
story.narration: And then the long haul north - over forest and lake and the widening fells, day after day, to the roof of Sweden.

story.title: The summer in Lapland
story.narration: At Akka's own mountain, Kebnekaise, above the glaciers in the Lapland light, the wild geese come down at last to nest and raise their young through the brief bright summer.

story.title: Summer ends
story.narration: The young geese are grown and can fly. When the first cold comes, the whole flock lifts from the fells and turns south - the long way home.

story.title: The whole way home
story.narration: When the summer ends the whole flock turns south again, down the length of the country the boy has now seen entire, home to the farm where it all began.

story.title: Home at last
story.narration: His parents are about to kill the fattened gander. Nils breaks cover to save Mårten - and the selfless act lifts the spell: he stands up a full-grown boy again, made kind, the journey closed exactly where it opened.
